item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went through most of the article thinking it was mildly interesting, but nothing special, but then that twist at the end secured my upvote. Well done.

item.title: Re: Change reverted
item.content:encoded: Perhaps. Are you sure you saw this exact usage, where it denotes "not in any way"? It's all a bit academic anyway, since it seems to be correct whether I like it or not. ;)

item.title: Re: Change reverted
item.content:encoded: I think it could be a British English thing? It's never seemed jarring to me and I've read it in a number of publications.

item.title: Re: Change reverted
item.content:encoded:
Pretty sure you're incorrectly interpreting that page. I'd be very interested to hear how the word's usage here is correct.

Never mind, I found what you're referring to. Regardless, it's usage here is actually pretty jarring. *shrug* YMMV apparently. :)

item.title: Change reverted
item.content:encoded:
Reverted a change made that had assumed incorrect word usage. The word usage was not incorrect.

See https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Underrated scip! This thing is truly amazing, short, sweet and original. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Many thanks! I enjoyed putting it together, and the idea scares me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe they get iterations, kind of like SCPs? something like SCP-XXXX-1 but for D Class it would be D-666-666?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this skip deserves more praise than it has right now. You did a good job on it
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the upvote, and the comments. I believe there is another 'cooldown'—or incubation—period before the SCP can reproduce, and they killed the earlier subjects too soon to see that.

It was not a typo; I prefer 'whatever' to 'whatsoever' and they can have equivalent meaning: see https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/whatever definition 2. I'm going to revert the change. Thanks for your attention, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just had a thought, though… wouldn't they have noticed the replicating thing way, way earlier? Like when they dissected the rat they used in the first test? Or does this (arbitrarily) only work on humans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed a typo; you had "whatever" where I'm fairly sure you meant "whatsoever." I honestly didn't like this at first - it seemed like a pretty standard doppelganger with some arthropod thrown in so as to be more sp00ky - but that final note really sold me. Take your upvote, it's well-earned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good catch. I'll stick it in. Glad you enjoyed it; I'm proud of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the article, +1 from me.
My only nitpick is if the D-class turned into SCP-1536-2 because of exposure…shouldn't there be a note about keeping Agent Rajesh under watch?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like to think we reuse designations like… some are cursed.. all D class feel sorry for D-666

"did you see the new 666?"
"yeah man fucking hot, i'd like to tap that'
"dude… d-666 always gets [redacted]"
"crying shame… a body like that."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyable SCP. The ending made me frown hard and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good science. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a clue what the photo is! I just got it from the SCP fuel repository. It was called 'estuary artefact'.

Glad you liked the article! Not enough to upvote, though…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Curious; what's the photo of? Looks like a scorched barrel?

I like the article. The feeling I got was of some kind of macrovirus, infecting humans by becoming them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[annoying pedantry]
Well, my understanding (for what it's worth ;-) ) is that an "infohazard" is something that is dangerous when you exchange and / or process information of a particular kind, thus, hazards that trigger when something "sees you" are infohazards too, since the object is threatening you whenever it gets visual information about you (and this SCP replaces people it "sees" with yet another SCP, so it probably qualifies)
[end of annoying pedantry]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It could be … I have tried a number of times to get my head round the classes of hazards (and by extension the tagging system), but I can't quite get it. It is my understanding that an infohazard is a thing information on which is dangerous (making the file on it be hazardous, for example), but I may well be wrong.

I'll certainly leave the tagging to someone else!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great stuff.
A proper twist ending, finally.

BTW, given that this thing "replaces" target as well as "mimics" target, does this mean it's a kind of infohazard (you see it, it mimics you, now both you and the mimic are skips)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not expect that ending. This is very disconcerting.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending's really good, presenting us with an identity crisis scenario and all, but the buildup is pretty uninteresting. I mean, they're just things that mimic moving stuff. Probably some potential here, not sure what you could do with it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They could be, in terms of the multiuniversal Foundation headcanon. Within the same universe, not possible; He turns into SCP-1536-2 in your article, and she is bludgeoned, turned inside out, and thrown into a star in mine.

Oh, well :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's the same guy? I'm rather fond of him, after all the time I've spent editing his dialogue for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upon reading "D-90124", I endured an abrupt heart-wrenching sense of déjà vu. Turns out I used the same D-Class designation within my SCP-2000 draft. goes off to edit sandbox

Aside from that, excellent article. Take my +1.

item.title: Second SCP
item.content:encoded: Thanks to the people in the chat - Warscribe, Nikolaus, Royalwolf, Bunton, and TwistedGears - who helped me, and the people on the forum - LongWayHome and thedeadlymoose - who provided criticism.
